reason:
Both answers reach the same correct core conclusion (don't match broadly, segment defense, expect their pricing to be unsustainable, use targeted retention offers) and both flag the same exception (truly undifferentiated product + price as top loss reason). They're comparable in structure and quality.

A has a couple of genuinely additional decision-relevant elements: (1) a rough quantification of the revenue hit (~$1.2M loss on $3M ARR if applied broadly), which helps the asker viscerally grasp the runway risk; (2) concrete numeric triggers for revisiting the strategy (churn >2-3%/mo, win-rate drop >20pts) rather than B's vaguer "if win rates and churn hold steady"; (3) an explicit strategic option to raise now from a position of strength while runway is intact — a nontrivial, actionable idea B doesn't mention. These aren't padding; they're concrete numbers and an extra option that could change what the asker actually does.

B is well-written and slightly more concise, but doesn't add any comparable new fact or option beyond what A already covers — its "lock in multi-year contracts" point is functionally the same as A's "case-by-case multi-yr lock-ins."

Given the extra concrete, decision-relevant content in A that isn't just restatement, A has a slight edge.
